result: The kettle whistles and the morning opens like an eyelid. I pour hot water over tea leaves. They swirl, a small, soft circle. Sunlight crawls across the floor, a quiet pet. My hands are still from last night’s dishes; I let them be. Outside, a breeze rummages the neighbor’s maple like a curious child, letting some leaves fall, then putting a few back. I think of places I’ve never been—the blue-lit cafés in Barcelona at noon, rain turning cobblestones into polished mirrors, or the train that winds through pine forests and you can almost taste the resin on your tongue. But the cup sits before me, steam rising like a thin, patient comet. My phone lights up with messages I pretend not to hear until later. The cat bats at the steam and I tell her it isn’t food, but she is unconvinced. I decide not to rush toward anything. I decide to be a place where time slows and then, like a stray dog, I decide to wander into it and see what it loves. The kettle has cooled. I make another cup because one is not enough, not when the room is learning to be mine. I keep walking.

But the cup sits before me, steam rising like a thin, patient comet. My phone lights up with messages I pretend not to hear until later. The cat bats at the steam and I tell her it isn’t food, but she is unconvinced. I decide not to rush toward anything. I decide to be a place where time slows and then, like a stray dog, I decide to wander into it and see what it loves. The kettle has cooled. I make another cup because one is not enough, not when the room is learning to be mine. I keep walking.
